metadata.title: AI Speeds You Up. What Does It Do to Your Skills?
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one earned accent per beat; mono for code/output only
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: German. Wagwan check: charsum('claude-liam-ai-skill-formation') % 10 != 0 — and Wagwan is Bear's alone; Liam never takes it.
metadata.note: claude-liam claude-explainer. LAWS: B00 = ClaudeComposerAsk cold open, IN-FOR-BEAR LAW: B00 first breath says 'this is Liam, in for Bear'. Figures 1-3 are REBUILT as Remotion scenes (AiSkillStudyDesign, AiSkillTreatmentEffect, AiSkillUsageModes) — never screenshots. ILLUSTRATE LAW: Claude UI only where the UI is the subject. HANDOFF second-to-last. OUTRO restates title. Source: Shen & Tamkin 2026 arXiv:2601.20245.
metadata.description_blurb: A randomized controlled trial gave half of 52 developers an AI assistant and half coded by hand. The AI group finished 2 minutes faster — not significant. They scored 17 percentage points lower on mastery — very significant. Liam takes the study apart.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A trial gave half of 52 developers an AI assistant on an unfamiliar async library; that group finished about two minutes faster. The speed gain was not significant, but quiz scores were fifty versus sixty-seven percent, nearly two letter grades apart. The biggest gap was in debugging, exactly the skill needed to oversee AI-written code. Delegation modes that offload thinking scored low, while comprehension and conceptual-inquiry modes built understanding at no cost to speed.

props.artifactTitle: How you use AI shapes your skills
artifactLines: A trial gave half of 52 developers an AI assistant on an unfamiliar async library; that group finished about two minutes faster.
The speed gain was not significant, but quiz scores were fifty versus sixty-seven percent, nearly two letter grades apart.
The biggest gap was in debugging, exactly the skill needed to oversee AI-written code.
Delegation modes that offload thinking scored low, while comprehension and conceptual-inquiry modes built understanding at no cost to speed.

beats.narration_text: The gap between offloading modes and understanding-building modes, delegation scoring under forty while conceptual inquiry cleared sixty-five, is what I want to apply to myself. I'll pick a library I've never used. Can you help me (1) design a generation-then-comprehension workflow instead of pure AI delegation, (2) build in the getting-painfully-stuck moments that the debugging-gap finding says I actually need, and (3) explain how conceptual inquiry stayed the second-fastest mode so learning cost me no speed?

props.segment: AI Speeds You Up. What Does It Do to Your Skills?
props.command: The gap between offloading modes and understanding-building modes, delegation scoring under forty while conceptual inquiry cleared sixty-five, is what I want to apply to myself. I'll pick a library I've never used. Can you help me (1) design a generation-then-comprehension workflow instead of pure AI delegation, (2) build in the getting-painfully-stuck moments that the debugging-gap finding says I actually need, and (3) explain how conceptual inquiry stayed the second-fastest mode so learning cost me no speed?

beats.narration_text: A randomized controlled trial. Fifty-two developers, all Python-experienced, none had touched the Trio async library before. Half got an AI assistant. Half coded by hand. Then both groups took a quiz on the concepts they had just used.
The AI group finished roughly two minutes faster. Panel A. Point estimates with confidence intervals. Overlapping. P equals 0.391. Not statistically significant.

Panel B. Quiz scores. Fifty percent for the AI group. Sixty-seven for the control. The confidence intervals do not overlap. P equals 0.010. Cohen's d of 0.738. Nearly two letter grades. And the biggest gap was in debugging — exactly the skill you need to oversee AI-written code.
But it is not whether you used AI. It is how. The researchers identified six interaction modes among the AI group. Three scored under forty percent. Three scored above sixty-five.
The low-scoring modes: AI delegation, progressive reliance, iterative AI debugging. All under forty percent. These offload the thinking. The AI does the problem-solving; the human watches.

props.sparkLine: Offload the thinking. Lose the skill.

beats.narration_text: The high-scoring modes: generation-then-comprehension, hybrid code-explanation, conceptual inquiry. All above sixty-five percent. These use AI to build understanding. And conceptual inquiry — asking the AI to explain the concepts, not just generate code — was the second fastest mode overall. Learning did not even cost them speed.

Getting painfully stuck is part of mastery. Claude Code has a Learning output style and an Explanatory output style — use them so you still understand what you shipped. Caveats stated plainly: n equals 52, the quiz came minutes after the task, long-term effects are unknown, and the study used a sidebar assistant, not an agentic tool like Claude Code. The design question for every session: are you learning, or just delegating?

artifactLines: Speed gap: ~2 min faster (AI). p = 0.391. Not significant.
Mastery gap: 50% vs 67%. Cohen’s d = 0.738. p = 0.010. Significant.
The variable is HOW, not WHETHER.
Low-skill modes offload thinking. High-skill modes build understanding.
Caveats: n=52, quiz minutes after task, sidebar not agentic.
The design question: are you learning, or just delegating?

beats.narration_text: Take this into your next session.
